mappings.rationaleAI: Garibaldi's military campaigns, Mazzini's Young Italy, Cavour's diplomacy, and Victor Emmanuel's role in Italian unification map directly to the Risorgimento outline entries (A.2.m.iii), the Italian War of Independence against Austria (B.2.a.ii), and the completion of unification through the conquest of the south and acquisition of Rome (B.3.f.i).

Lenin's role in the 1917 Russian Revolution and establishment of Bolshevik governance directly addresses the abolition of the monarchy in Russia (B.3.e.iv), while the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk and Russia's military collapse connect to the WWI narrative of the Eastern Front and the armistice (B.2.d.iii).

Nelson's 1805 naval victory destroying the Franco-Spanish fleet and establishing British naval supremacy maps directly to the Third Coalition battles including Trafalgar and Austerlitz (A.1.c.iii), and the failure of Napoleon's Continental System.

The enormous financial cost of French intervention in the American Revolution burdened the French treasury and contributed to the fiscal crisis precipitating the 1789 French Revolution, directly connecting to the climate of revolutionary change preceding 1789 (A.1.a).

The 1815 Corn Laws, the Anti-Corn Law League led by Cobden and Bright, and repeal in 1846 under Peel splitting the Conservative Party map directly to post-Napoleonic economic depression and social unrest (A.2.i.i) and the era of political and social reform including repeal of the Corn Laws (A.2.i.ii).

Pan-European revolutionary upheavals of 1848 in France, Germany, Austria, Hungary, and Italy map directly to the revolutions of 1848 and their suppression (A.2.h.ii), the French Second Republic (A.2.j.iii), the Frankfurt National Assembly (A.2.k.ii), revolutionary uprisings in Vienna, Prague, and Budapest (A.2.l.iii), and the first phase of the Italian War of Independence (A.2.m.iv).

The social and intellectual consequences of British industrialization -- changes in lifestyles, health, housing, labour relations, and the theories developed to address urban living and mass production -- directly address the social consequences of the Industrial Revolution (A.2.a.iii).

Luxemburg's anti-war activism, co-founding of the Spartacus League and German Communist Party, and murder during the 1919 Spartacist uprising connect to Social Democrats and militarists in pre-WWI Germany (B.3.c.iv) and the establishment of the German Republic in 1919 (B.3.c.v).

The 1814-1815 Congress redrawing the European map under Metternich, Castlereagh, and Talleyrand maps directly to the Congress of Vienna and Metternich's attempt to restore the old order (A.1.c.v), and the subsequent Congress system for maintaining the Vienna settlement (A.2.h).

The Prussian siege of Paris after Napoleon III's defeat at Sedan, the establishment and bloody suppression of the Paris Commune, and the founding of the Third Republic map directly to Napoleon III's defeat by Germany (B.3.b.i) and the siege, the Commune, and establishment of the Third Republic (B.3.b.ii).
Alexander II's 1861 abolition of serfdom following defeat in the Crimean War maps directly to the Crimean War defeat, emancipation of serfs, and local government reforms under Alexander II (B.3.e.i).

Bismarck's wars against Denmark, Austria, and France creating the German Empire (B.2.a.iii), his clashes with liberals and army reform (B.3.c.i), the Schleswig-Holstein question and exclusion of Austria (B.3.c.ii), and his chancellorship including Kulturkampf, anti-Socialist measures, and the Triple Alliance (B.3.c.iii) are all directly covered.

Hindenburg's hero status from the 1914 Battle of Tannenberg on the Eastern Front and promotion of the stab-in-the-back myth after Germany's defeat connect directly to WWI opening campaigns (B.2.d.i) and the leadership and strategic goals of the belligerents (B.2.d.v).

The 1792 battle where revolutionary citizen volunteers defeated Prussian-Austrian forces, followed by the abolition of the monarchy and declaration of the First Republic, maps directly to the uprising of the Third Estate and abolition of the monarchy (A.1.b.i) and the War of the First Coalition (A.1.c.i).

Bismarck's 1884-1885 conference establishing rules for claiming African territory and accelerating the partition of Africa maps directly to renewed European interest in overseas expansion (B.2.c.i) and the scramble for Africa (B.2.c.ii).

The 1851 Great Exhibition in the Crystal Palace, showcasing British industrial supremacy and free trade ideology under Prince Albert's vision, maps directly to mid-Victorian politics and economics including the Great Exhibition (B.3.a.i).

The 1791-1804 Haitian Revolution was triggered directly by French Revolutionary ideals of liberty colliding with plantation slavery, involving French republicans and British/Spanish military interventions, connecting to France's support for revolution in neighbouring lands and the international dimensions of the Revolutionary Wars (A.1.c.i).

The 1871 Algerian insurrection triggered partly by France's Franco-Prussian War defeat exemplifies European colonial expansion and its consequences in Africa (B.2.c.i), with French military suppression followed by accelerated settler colonization.

Besant's championing of workers' rights, the 1888 match factory strike, Irish Home Rule, and Fabian socialism connect directly to the growth of the Labour movement, Fabian socialism, and the Irish Home Rule struggle in late Victorian Britain (B.3.a.iii).

Goethe's literary career spanning Sturm und Drang, Weimar Classicism, and early Romanticism, his partnership with Schiller, and his support for Napoleon map directly to the Romantic movement (A.2.c) and German Idealism and philosophy including Kant's disciples (A.2.e), as a towering figure bridging these intellectual currents.

The Dreyfus Affair exposing antisemitism and political divisions in the Third Republic, Zola's J'Accuse, and the resulting 1905 separation of church and state map directly to the Republican Republic, the Dreyfus affair, and separation of church and state (B.3.b.ii).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Economic Consequences of the Peace
mappings.rationaleAI: Keynes's critique of the Versailles Treaty's punitive reparations, written after his resignation from the British delegation at the Paris Peace Conference, maps directly to the peace treaty, Wilson's influence, German reparations and restrictions, and reorganization of Europe (B.2.d.vi).

The British Empire's expansion from 1877 to its peak in 1919, covering political, economic, and ideological motivations for imperial expansion, maps to the resurgence of European imperialism (B.2.c) and British overseas expansion under Disraeli and subsequent governments (B.3.a).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Zulu Nation's Rise and Fall
mappings.rationaleAI: The 1879 Battle of Isandhlwana and British conquest of the Zulu kingdom into the colony of Natal exemplify the European penetration and partitioning of Africa during the scramble for colonial territories (B.2.c.ii).

Lawrence's role in the WWI Arab Revolt against Ottoman rule connects to campaigns in the Middle East (B.2.d.ii), while his disillusionment with the postwar settlement and British betrayal of Arab self-determination connects to the Paris Peace Conference's reorganization of the Middle East (B.2.d.vi).

The concept of sovereignty as practically tested and redefined by the French Revolution -- transferring sovereign authority from monarchs to the people -- connects directly to the revolutionary transformation of French government (A.1.b) and the legacy of the French Revolution including cultural nationalism and populism (A.2.b).
The 19th-century European archaeological rivalry in Egypt and Mesopotamia was driven by imperialist expansion and nationalism (B.2.e imperialist expansion and colonialism in the late 19th century).

The poem addresses Victorian social issues including reform, illegitimacy, and women's struggle, connecting to developments in arts and philosophy (B.1.b) and political and social reform measures in mid-19th century Britain (A.2.i.ii).

Arminius (Hermann) became a rallying point for 19th-century German nationalism (B.2.d unification of Germany), connecting ancient military history with cultural nationalism (A.2.b legacy of cultural nationalism).

Disraeli served as Prime Minister during the era of British politics from 1850-1920 (B.2.f Great Britain), and his political career connects to earlier political and social reform in 19th-century Britain (A.2.i.ii).

Bergson's fame rivaling Einstein and his influence on Proust, Woolf, and the Cubists places him in European cultural and intellectual life from 1850-1920 (B.1.a philosophy and political thought, B.1.b developments in the arts).

Chartism's demands for political reform including universal male suffrage and voting by secret ballot are directly covered under political and social reform measures in Britain 1822-48, including the Chartist movement (A.2.i.ii).
Chekhov chronicled changing Russian society in the late 19th century, connecting to developments in European arts (B.1.b) and Russian social and political conditions (B.2.o Russia).

The poem directly depicts Europe ravaged by the Napoleonic Wars and revolutionary upheaval (A.1.c), reflects the legacy of the French Revolution including cultural nationalism (A.2.b), and exemplifies the Romantic movement's individualism and concern for nature (A.2.c).

Crime and Punishment is set in 1860s St Petersburg during the Russian Empire under Alexander II, a period of reform and revolutionary sentiment (B.2.e.i), and reflects the economic, cultural, and social developments of the era (B.2.e.iii).
Dadaism began in 1916 as a direct artistic response to the absurdity of World War I (B.2.d), protesting the war while experimenting with radical forms across European cities.
Emma was published in 1815 during the Regency era, reflecting the social world of Great Britain from 1789 to 1815 (A.1.e) at the close of the Napoleonic period.
Hopkins's life (1844-1889) falls within Victorian-era European cultural and economic life (B.1), and his posthumous 1918 publication bridges 19th-century Romanticism with 20th-century modernism.
Conrad's work (1899) critiques European imperialism during the high period of European cultural and economic life from 1850 to 1920 (B.1).

Clare's protest against enclosure during the Agricultural Revolution connects to pan-European economic and industrial developments (A.2), and his life (1793-1864) spans the period of European cultural and economic transformation (B.1).
Keats (1795-1821) was part of the English Romantic movement during pan-European cultural developments in the first half of the 19th century (A.2).
Modernism emerged from the cultural rupture of World War I and was influenced by European intellectual currents including Nietzsche and Freud, within European cultural life from 1850 to 1920 (B.1).
Flaubert's critique of bourgeois provincial life in mid-19th-century France connects to European cultural life from 1850 to 1920 (B.1).
Wollstonecraft witnessed the French Revolution firsthand in Paris, and her work was published in 1792 in direct response to revolutionary ideals (A.1 on the French Revolution and its effects).
The episode is set against World War I (B.2.d covers the war), and Sassoon's public denunciation and war experience relate to British participation in WWI (B.3.a.iv/v).

Spartacus became a symbolic hero for 19th-century European revolutionary and socialist movements, connecting to the Romantic movement and cultural nationalism as legacy of the French Revolution (A.2.c).

The episode connects to the influence of the French Revolution on English radicalism and governmental hostility to reform (A.1.e), as the Gordon Riots foreshadowed the crowd violence and political instability of the revolutionary era.

Munch's work emerged from late 19th-century European cultural developments in the arts, philosophy, and religion (B.1.b), reflecting fin-de-siecle existential concerns and the transition from Symbolism to Expressionism.
The later Romantics epitomized the Romantic movement's individualism, emphasis on imagination, and rebellion against convention (A.2.c).

Mill's philosophical and political activity falls within 19th-century European intellectual life (A.2.e on philosophy); his advocacy for franchise extension connects to mid-Victorian reform movements (B.3.a.ii).
The First Reform Bill of 1832 is explicitly covered under political and social reform measures in Britain 1822-48, including the First Reform Bill (A.2.i.ii).
Monet's London series represents developments in the arts during the late 19th and early 20th centuries (B.1.b), specifically Impressionism's evolution toward greater abstraction.

Persuasion's Regency-era setting reflects early 19th-century British social conditions (A.2.i) including class consciousness and tensions between landed gentry and naval meritocracy, within the broader Romantic cultural context (A.2.c).
Kipling's work addresses the British Empire during the era of resurgent European imperialism (B.2.c) and late Victorian/Edwardian Britain (B.3.a.iv).
Wells's satire of Victorian class separation between leisured elites and industrial labourers connects to late 19th-century European cultural life (B.1.a) and economic industrialization (B.1.d).

The Clearances spanning the 18th-19th centuries in Scotland relate to British social conditions after the Napoleonic Wars (A.2.i) and the broader transformations of Great Britain during the era of industrialization and reform (B.3.a).
The Romantic movement's emphasis on individualism, nature, and emotion as counterweights to Enlightenment rationalism and industrial change is directly covered (A.2.c).
The collapse of constitutional home rule, the Easter Rising of 1916, and World War I are covered under British participation in WWI and the Easter Rising (B.3.a.iv/v).

Wilde's arrest and imprisonment for gross indecency reflects Victorian social mores and legal persecution, situated in the cultural and social developments of late 19th-century Britain (B.3.a, B.3.b).
The poem reflects mid-Victorian intellectual crisis and culture, situated in the broader social and cultural developments of 19th-century Britain (B.3.a, B.3.b).

The rebellion was directly inspired by the French and American revolutions (A.1.a climate of change), and the French revolutionary wars saw a small French expeditionary force sent to Ireland (A.1.b.i).

Wilberforce's abolitionist campaign spans the era of the French Revolution and Napoleonic period (A.1.b.i) through to the reform era of 19th-century Britain including labour movements (A.2.a) and the 1807 and 1833 abolition acts (B.3.a).

Napoleon's 1812 Russian campaign and catastrophic retreat, the collapse of the Continental System, and the subsequent defeat and exile directly correspond to the Franco-Austrian war and Napoleon's invasion of Russia (A.1.c.iv), the Congress of Vienna and post-Napoleonic settlement (A.1.c.v), and France under the Napoleonic Empire (A.1.b.iv).

De Gouges's Declaration of the Rights of Woman (1791) and her execution during the Terror (1793) are directly situated within the French Revolution: the reforms of the Constituent Assembly (A.1.b.i) and the Terror under the Convention (A.1.b.ii).

The Vampyre arose from the Villa Diodati sessions in 1816 alongside Shelley's Frankenstein, situated within Romantic-era literary culture and the broader cultural developments of early 19th-century Europe (B.3.a).
Victorian sensation fiction of the 1860s reflects anxieties about gender, class, and respectability in mid-Victorian British society and culture (B.3.a, B.3.b).

The Kalevala's 1835 publication catalyzed the Finnish national awakening, elevating the Finnish language and contributing to Finland's independence movement, situating it within 19th-century European nationalism (B.3.a, B.3.b).

Wordsworth's experience in revolutionary France connects to the climate of change during the French Revolution (A.1.a), and the poem's place in English Romanticism connects to broader 19th-century cultural developments (B.3.a).

The novel is set during the years preceding the Reform Act of 1832 and portrays social hierarchy, politics, and the conditions of English life in that period, directly corresponding to political and social reform measures in Britain 1815-50 (A.2.i.ii).

Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo in 1815 and the preceding Peninsular War where Wellington commanded British forces correspond directly to the Franco-Austrian War, Peninsular War, Napoleon's defeat (A.1.c.iv) and the Hundred Days and Waterloo (A.1.c.v). Contrasting military strategies and leadership styles of the two commanders.

Napoleon's escape from Elba, displacement of Louis XVIII, near-victory and ultimate defeat at Waterloo, and exile to Saint Helena directly correspond to the Hundred Days and Napoleon's final defeat at Waterloo (A.1.c.v), the Directory and rise of Napoleon (A.1.b.iii), and France under the Napoleonic Empire (A.1.b.iv). Napoleon's memoir-writing on Saint Helena to shape his legacy connects to the broader political mythology of the Napoleonic era.

Burns's influence on the Romantic poets and his work's embodiment of individualism and concern for nature and folk tradition connect to the Romantic movement as a pan-European cultural development (A.2.c).

The novel depicts late-nineteenth-century rural England, agrarian decline, class exploitation, and the tensions between literary realism and Victorian propriety, connecting to developments in the arts, philosophy, and religion in the period 1850-1920 (B.1.b).

The debate in Victorian Britain between Dickens and Darwin/Mill over Governor Eyre's actions, and the wider questions of colonial governance and racial justice, connect to European imperial and colonial politics in the latter half of the 19th century (B.2.h).

The Norman Yoke concept fueled radical and democratic movements from the Levellers through the Chartists, framing resistance to aristocratic power as the recovery of ancient rights, connecting to political and social reform movements in Britain 1815-50 (A.2.i.ii).

The Peterloo Massacre (1819) directly connects to economic depression and social unrest following the Napoleonic Wars and governmental repression (A.2.i.i), and its role in galvanising the reform movement and contributing to the Great Reform Act of 1832 connects to political and social reform measures in Britain 1815-50 (A.2.i.ii).

The novel captured the deteriorating Anglo-German relations in the early 1900s, connecting to the formation of the Triple Entente and realignment of European alliances (B.2.b.ii) and the prelude to World War I including the crises and outbreak of war between the great powers (B.2.b.iii).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
mappings.rationaleAI: The poem, composed in collaboration with Wordsworth in 1798, is a defining work of the Romantic movement's concern with nature, the supernatural, and individual experience (A.2.c).

The 1859 publication and subsequent Victorian popularity occurred during the Romantic/post-Romantic cultural movements (A.2.c); the Pre-Raphaelites who championed it were part of British cultural life in the latter nineteenth century (B.3.e)

The statue was conceived in France as a gift commemorating Franco-American revolutionary friendship; its design reflects the legacy of the French Revolution and republican ideals (A.1.b); Bartholdi and Eiffel's engineering achievement represents nineteenth-century European cultural and technological accomplishment (B.3.e)

The novel was published in 1848 during the height of Victorian literary culture; its unflinching portrayal of alcoholism, marital abuse, and women's legal powerlessness reflects the social concerns of mid-nineteenth-century Britain (B.3.e - Great Britain cultural and social developments)

Published in 1922, the poem captured postwar spiritual desolation after World War I; it reflects the cultural crisis following the catastrophe of the Great War (C.5 World War I); the poem's break with Victorian conventions represents the broader cultural transformation of early twentieth-century Europe (C.6.a)

The abolitionist movement galvanized by the Zong case became part of the broader reform movements in late eighteenth and early nineteenth-century Britain (A.1.e.i - the influence of the French Revolution on English radicalism and reform)

Tocqueville asked whether European societies could adopt democratic principles without sacrificing liberty, writing against the backdrop of post-revolutionary European political thought (A.2.b legacy of the French Revolution; A.2.c the Romantic movement and individualism)

Alexander II's 1881 assassination by Narodnaya Volya occurred during the era of reform and reaction in Russia; his emancipation of the serfs (1861) and modernizing reforms relate to Russian developments in the latter nineteenth century (B.3.f); the revolutionary populist movement and its consequences for Russian political trajectory toward 1917 (C.2.a)

Victorian pessimism pervaded an era of industrial progress and imperial confidence in nineteenth-century Britain; anxieties about religious faith, racial degeneration, and moral decline coexisted with technological optimism (B.3.e Great Britain social and cultural developments); the broader European intellectual ferment of the late nineteenth century (B.5)

Van Gogh worked in the late nineteenth century, producing roughly nine hundred paintings in his final decade; his career and its posthumous recognition belong to the cultural developments of late nineteenth-century Europe (B.5)

Wagner's republican political activity during the 1849 Dresden uprising relates to the revolutionary movements of 1848-49 in Germany (B.3.c); his operas' themes connect to the Romantic movement's individualism (A.2.c)
Owen was killed one week before the Armistice in November 1918; his poems like Dulce et Decorum Est convey the horror of trench warfare on the Western Front during World War I (C.5)

Published in 1847, the novel belongs to the extraordinary flowering of Victorian fiction; its moral ambiguity unsettled contemporaries, reflecting the tensions within Victorian literary culture (B.3.e)
